observations.managementAction:
The IAS commenced the implementation of the COA Circular No. 2018-003, on the adaptation of IASPPS and ICSPPS. In fact, last 
3rd quarter of 2023, the IAS conducted a review relative to the Management and Operations Audit of property, plant and equipment and inventory-related processes. Follow-up audit may be conducted, if necessary to ascertain compliance to the audit findings and recommendations as well as to measure the effectiveness of the implemented internal controls. 

Last January 2024, the Internal Audit Manual of the City was already created and approved for its adoption and implementation. This will aid to improve the effectiveness of governance, risk management and control processes of the City.


The GSD is continuously coordinating with IAS to improve the monitoring tools of inventory and updating of property records. In fact, the GSD is in the process of complying the recommendations and formulating the internal guidelines to strengthen the effectiveness of governance, risk management and control process for inventories and property, plant and equipment.  The GSD is further required to simplify the inventory process, so as to comply with   RA 
No. 11032, also known as Ease of Doing Business and Efficient Government Service Delivery Act of 2018.  It also includes establishment of a comprehensive system of monitoring transactions for an efficient turn-around in the delivery of government service, and a three-signatory rule for accountability purposes.  

Reconciliation was made as part of the year-end closing procedures. Causes of some variances were identified and supported with proper documentations. While the others represent items, which were recorded back as inventory despite submission of proofs of issuances from prior periods, since these were presented during the physical count. Yet, there is a need to revisit previous documentations to substantiate proper recording.

Moreover, asset management system including the use of the centralized warehouse are part of the initiatives of the City to strengthen the internal control system. This will help the City to safeguard its resources and to ensure that all inventory records are kept up to date and accurately recorded in the City’s Books of Accounts. Once the centralized warehouse was turned over to the GSD, it will enable the City to optimize the benefits and advantages of centralized warehousing to avoid the recurrence of audit observations issued by the COA. Currently, the City is looking for a warehouse to be used as storage for unserviceable properties to vacate the centralized warehouse and used for its intended purpose.

The GSD and Accounting Department did a reconciliation on a per account basis regularly.



To properly account for the receipts and issuances of supplies and materials, Inventory Reports are submitted to CAD on a monthly basis. This is an effective tool in updating the inventory records on a regular basis.

The CAD assures that Journal Vouchers (JV) relative to the receipts and issuances of inventories are supported with complete and valid documents.


Supplies Ledger Cards are regularly updated whenever there are transactions being recorded in the books of accounts.


The CAD in coordination with GSD will determine if there are adjustments and recognize the same in the books of accounts once identified.


The GSD and CAD already informed the supply and property officers/custodians to regularly update the SCs whenever there are receipts and/or issuances of inventories. Moreover, to ensure the efficient and effective updating of SCs, this is incorporated in the Asset Management System.


The submission of Monthly Inventory Report (MIR) accompanied by AIR/DR, RIS/RSMI and other similar documents is already incorporated in the existing inventory process.  Based on our existing process, once the delivery of supplies was received by the office/ department concerned, the Requisition Issuance Slip (RIS) was prepared by the GSD and subsequently forwarded to the CAD. Also, the timely submission of MIR was incorporated in the Office Performance Commitment Review to ensure strict compliance on the audit recommendation.

The City strengthened its monitoring through the submission of MIR with complete supporting documents (e.g. Notice of Delivery, Purchase Order, Requisition and Issue Slip, etc.) of all departments/ offices.
observations.reason: Verification and reconciliation of the RPCI and accounting were not fully completed due to incomplete documents pending review.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management direct the:

a.	IAS to implement COA Circular No. 2018-003, on the adoption of IASPPS and ICSPPS, to improve effectiveness of governance, risk management and control process of the City;






b. GSD, CAD and Inventory Committee (IC) to coordinate and conduct reconciliation of the results of physical inventory and accounting records; identify and address the nature and causes of variances; and hold responsible/ accountable the designated supply officers/ custodians pursuant to Section 491 of GAAM, Vol. I, and other existing laws, rules and regulations, and henceforth, keep up reconciled reports at all times;

c. IC to:

•	Prepare the List of PPEs Found at Station to facilitate the recognition of PPE items not recorded in the books to establish reasonable basis to consider the same as property of the City;

•	Prepare the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs to facilitate the conduct of required procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without established accountability; and

•	Strictly comply with the guidelines and procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to come up with reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts presented in the City’s financial statements.

d. GSD to:

•	Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with PARs as prescribed under Section 5.6 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; 
•	Renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever there is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability; and

•	Prepare the IIRUP for all PPEs found unserviceable, obsolete and/or no longer needed properties and facilitate immediate disposal thereof pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445.

e. CAD to:

•	Record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation with GSD to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts; and

•	Strictly comply with COA Circular Nos. 2018-003 and 2020-006, and Section 491, GAAM, Vol. I.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Legal Officer to take appropriate legal action against the Accountable Officers of the long outstanding cash advances amounting to P3,676,898.47 pursuant to the request, information and evidence provided by the City Accounting Office.

We further recommended the City Accountant to continue to ensure that current year’s cash advances and those granted in the last nine years are properly documented and monitored as to compliance with existing COA rules and regulations on the grant and liquidation of cash advances to avoid dormancy of the accounts.

We commended Management for its proactive efforts in significantly reducing the balance of RTH Claims, achieved through continuous communication and effective collaboration with both PhilHealth and OsMak personnel.

Going forward, we recommended the Director of Ospital ng Makati to continuously explore all possible remedies for the recovery/reimbursement of the hospital fees from PhilHealth totaling P37,878,738.55, representing the RTH as of December 31, 2024, and Denied Claims recorded for the current year to minimize the potential utilization of funds of the City through donations.

Furthermore, we recommended that the City Accountant reassess the accounting treatment and make the necessary adjustments to the "Due from PhilHealth," "Donations," and other related "Fees" accounts to correct the respective understatements and overstatements of these accounts.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer ensure that all PPAs proposed to be funded under the LDRRMF are thoroughly evaluated prior to their final inclusion in the LDRRMF Investment Plan, giving due consideration to the PPAs’ feasibility, necessity and exigency to minimize, if not totally prevent cancellations or reprogramming of projects and delays in their implementation so that the benefits intended to be derived from the completed PPAs can be enjoyed by the City’s constituents in a timely manner.

We recommended the Urban Development Department  to institute control measures to ensure that the data and information in the electronic copy of CC expenditures tagged Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and those encoded from AIP Form to CCET Analysis Tool are accurate and complete prior to submission to the CCC to facilitate informed decision-making by concerned city and/or national government officials on matters concerning identification, prioritization, monitoring, evaluation, reporting of, and budgeting for CC PPAs.

We recommended that the Makati Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) Core Team oversee the process of tagging climate change responsive PPAs to ensure the appropriateness and consistency of the identified and prioritized climate PPAs.

We recommended the City Accounting Department and the HRDO to:
Continue to comply with the relevant provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 8291 by ensuring the timely remittance of valid employee and government agency contributions and deductions to the GSIS within the first ten days of the calendar month following the month in which the contributions and deductions were made; and
Promptly remit the remaining balance of contributions and other deductions for CY 2024 amounting to P2.886 million, after thorough validation and clarification with GSIS.

We recommended that the CAD and CTD consistently withhold the required taxes from personnel compensation, as well as from payments to suppliers and contractors, and ensure timely remittance to the BIR.

We also advised the City Treasurer, as the withholding agent and liable officer, to confer with the BIR officials to discuss and potentially appeal any surcharges resulting in the late remittance of withheld taxes.

We commended the Makati City School Board (MCSB) and DepEd-Makati for having maintained a high utilization rate of 96.42 percent as to the approved budget indicating that the preparation and evaluation of aligned PPAs are viable.

We further recommended that Management, thru the MCSB, Education Department, to immediately coordinate with the Schools Division Superintendent and other concerned school personnel to establish the extent of the impact of the implementation of the new DepEd policy on the preparation and submission of supporting documents required by the CAD for the payment of obligations, and on the basis thereof, propose concrete measures to resolve the issues, including but not limited to hiring/assigning local personnel to support the school property custodians in carrying out various administrative tasks.

We recommended that the Management require the DEPW to:
Strictly monitor the completeness of information being posted in the tarpaulins/signboards displayed at the locations of various infrastructure projects in compliance with pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004, to encourage and/or promote public awareness and ensure accountability and/or transparency of the City in the implementation of its infrastructure projects; and
Inform this Office within 10 days after the award of infrastructure projects or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted, for monitoring and validation purposes.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly enforce the submission of required documents on the suspended transactions and/or claims/payments to avoid the issuance of NDs; and
Demand for the immediate settlement of the disallowed transactions or require the persons liable to avail of the payment.

We recommended the following:

a. 	The Head of the Billing and Claims Division of the OsMak to strictly implement all procedural policies and guidelines on the assessment, review, and filing of claims with PHIC to ensure that all claims for hospital fees are duly supported with complete documentations. Moreover, provide continuous trainings and seminars to all personnel in charge of the processing of PhilHealth claims to apprise themselves on the implementing rules and regulations of RA No. 7874, prevailing PHIC Circulars, and best practices relevant to the processing of PHIC claims;






b. 	The Director of OsMak to explore all possible means and remedies for the recovery /reimbursement of hospital fees from PhilHealth totaling P214.431 million. Make representation with the officials of the PhilHealth through filing a motion for reconsideration to allow the re-filing of complied RTH and Denied Claims despite overdue so that recorded income from hospital fees will be realized/collected.

c. 	The management also may consider the option to make representation with the PHIC for possible extension of PHIC office in OsMak to assist in the filing of PHIC claims.  In such manner, all claims for hospital fees will undergo proper screening by PHIC personnel to ensure its validity/worthiness for reimbursement.
observations.managementAction:
The OsMak to have annual review of the Inter-Departmental guidelines for PHIC claims. So far, below are the courses of actions that were already implemented and taken into consideration:

Creation of team (consultants, residents and PhilHealth personnel) assigned to answer queries pertaining to PHIC claims
Resumption of PHIC ward clerk to follow-up completion of PHIC requirements as well as the validation team to check the requirements prior to patient’s discharge
Thorough checking of PHIC claims prior to submission and encoding
Started Electronic Member Registration and Records Amendment (EMRRA) for the enrollment of patients to PHIC and updating the patient’s record
Constant communication with PHIC local and regional representatives for clarification and possible solution of problematic PHIC claims

To further familiarize on the prevailing PHIC circulars, resident doctors and personnel in charge attended several trainings and updates, as follows:  

Resumption of yearly training of resident doctors regarding PHIC CF4 completion and proper PHIC diagnosis with International Classification of Diseases (ICD)/Relative Value Scale (RVS) codes
Billing and Claims personnel were sent for ICD training
Constantly updating the clinical division and billing and claims personnel regarding latest PHIC circulars and updates through written communication and/or meetings

Filed for motion for reconsideration to the denied claims with justification from the Clinical Division. Moreover, additional manpower was assigned to facilitate RTH and denied PHIC claims.



A letter was sent to PhilHealth requesting for an officer to be stationed in OsMak to enhance the processing of claims.  However, the PhilHealth denied the request for PHIC extension office at OsMak. As a matter of policy, PhilHealth does not station an officer or any personnel in a hospital, except for PCARES (PhilHealth Customer Assistance, Relation Empowerment Staff) and Malasakit Center in Department of Health retained hospital. 

It was then recommended that OsMak may send personnel in charge in the processing of claims to the PhilHealth branch office at Makati City for orientation, retooling and updating purposes.

observations.recommendation:
COA recognized the perennial issue on the existence of enormous amounts of discrepancies in the PPE accounts of government agencies causing the non-establishment of the accuracy of PPE balances in their financial statements.  As such, COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, was issued providing guidelines and procedures to assist government agencies in coming up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

The procedural guidelines on the six main activities for one-time cleansing of PPE balances, namely the preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking, inventory taking, reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records, disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs, procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability, and accounting entries and disclosures in the notes to financial statements are enumerated under Items 6.1, 6.2, 6.3, 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0, respectively, of the above Circular. Pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 are set forth hereunder:

5.0 General Guidelines
5.9 	In coordination with the Property Division/Unit, the Inventory Committee shall plan/strategize on how to conduct and complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period. It shall prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing, at the least, the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory.
5.10 	The PIP shall be approved by the Head of the Agency.
5.11	The Inventory Committee shall submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities.
5.12	Property records shall be updated based on the results of the physical inventory and reconciled with accounting records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the agency's PPEs.”
6.0 Procedural Guidelines
6.1 	Preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking
6.1.3	The Property and Accounting Units shall compare the latest Report on the RPCPPE/Inventory Report with the PPELCs.
6.1.4	The Property Unit shall prepare a list of PPE items which are recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report as well as PPE items which are included in the RPCPPE but not recorded in the PPELCs.
6.1.5 	Both lists and the latest RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report shall be submitted to the Inventory Committee. These documents shall be considered as inventory working papers and shall be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the PIP.
6.2 Inventory taking
6.2.5	All PPE items counted shall be tagged with new property stickers containing the information provided under Paragraph 5.7 of this Circular.
6.2.6	The Inventory Committee shall state clearly in the ICF the condition of the PPEs, such as in good condition, needing repair, unserviceable, obsolete, no longer need, not used since purchase, etc.
6.2.7	PPEs found at station or items not included in the inventory working papers, but there is reasonable basis to consider the same as owned by the agency, shall likewise be included in the physical count and tagged with property stickers. These items shall be described as “found at station” which shall be indicated under the “Remarks” column of the ICF.
6.2.8	PPEs included in the inventory working papers but are not found during the physical count shall be considered as non-existing/missing PPEs per physical count. These items shall be described as “non-existing” or “missing” which shall indicated under the “Remarks” column of the ICF.
6.3 	Reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records
The Property and Accounting Units shall undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled. The reconciliation shall be completed within ten (10) days from rendition of the RPCPPE by the Inventory Committee. xxx
6.3.1 	The Property Unit shall:
Ensure that the PCs are updated by posting all unrecorded acquisition/receipt, issue/transfer and disposal of PPE, if any;
Prepare a List of PPEs Found at Station for those items described as " found at station" in the RPCPPE, using the format in Annex B of this Circular, and submit to the Accounting Unit for recording in the books of accounts;
Prepare PCs for items of PPEs found at station;
Prepare a List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs for items described as "non-existing" or "missing" in the RPCPPE as well as for those with PCs and Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs) on file but not included in the RPCPPE, following the format in Annex C of this Circular;
Follow the procedures for the disposition of non-existing/missing PPEs provided under Paragraph 7 of this Circular;
Work together with the Accounting Unit to reconcile the PCs with the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit;
Renew all PARs; and
Prepare Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IlRUP) using the format prescribed under the Accounting Manuals of the respective Sectors for all PPEs found unserviceable, obsolete and/or no longer needed.
6.3.2 The Accounting Unit shall:
Take up the necessary accounting entries to recognize PPEs found at station and prepare/maintain corresponding PPELCs based on the List of PPEs Found at Station;
Take up the necessary accounting entries to recognize loss of PPE and to set up the corresponding receivables from the concerned accountable officer/personnel, pursuant to Paragraphs 7.8 and 7.9 of this Circular; 7
Work together with the Property Unit in reconciling the PCs with the PPELCs maintained by the Property Unit;
Update the PPELCs as necessary in the course of reconciliation;
Take up the necessary accounting entries to write-off/drop from the books of accounts the remaining balances as of December 31, xxx; and
Ensure that the total balance of PPELCs/SLs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger.
observations.managementAction:
The Audit Team commend the Inventory Committee (IC) for conducting and completing the physical inventory-taking of the properties of the City. Nevertheless, we observed that there were some lapses in the inventory-taking, and an unreconciled balance of P731.690 million between RPCPPE and accounting records as shown in the next table.

The IC was able to conduct the physical inventory-taking of properties and prepare the RPCPPE for CY 2024. However, reconciliation was not adequately made between the RPCPPE and the accounting records in accordance with the aforecited guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to resolve the above noted variance of P731,690,134.56. Consequently, the non-reconciliation of the results of physical inventory with the GSD and the CAD records casted doubt on the validity, existence, completeness and accuracy of the balances of PPE accounts presented in the financial statements of the City.

As per Management, the GSD, the CAD, and the IC are continuously coordinating with each other in the reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records so that the reasons for the discrepancy will be identified. The reconciliation of PPE accounts per book and per physical count was being done all year-round. However, the reconciliation was tedious considering that the volume of unreconciled PPE accounts pertained to those acquired prior to CY 2012, and the limitations on the availability of the documents on file.

Our review of the on-going cleansing activities of PPE balances of the City further disclosed the following:
The IC was able to submit a copy of the PIP and the Schedule of Physical Count containing the dates of inventory-taking; names of IC members who will conduct the count; and the location/offices where the inventory were to be conducted;
Not all PPE items counted were tagged with pre-numbered property stickers or temporary control stickers for efficiency and effectiveness of physical inventory. Thus, PPE could not be easily determined, and the risks of omissions and errors of miscounting may not have been prevented;
The List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs, to facilitate the disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs and the List of PPEs Found at Station during the actual count, to facilitate the recognition thereof in the books of accounts, were not consistently prepared;
Various PPE items listed in the submitted RPCPPE were without property number or control reference. Actual condition of various PPE was also not completely described or filled out. Thus, the accountability, control over the custody/use and actual condition of the City’s assets could not be ascertained.
As per Management, the CAD, the GSD, and the IC started the activities for the one-time cleansing to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. The GSD is in the process of consolidating the PPE found to be unserviceable, obsolete, and/or no longer needed. The City has a continuous disposal of supplies and properties through the Committee on Awards and Disposal. As to PAR, the City issues PAR whenever a new PPE is acquired. For CY 2022, the City strictly complied with the prescribed format and numbering system for issued PAR. The GSD is continuously updating all the PAR already prescribed and transferring it to the new accountable officers. The PAR numbers will then be indicated upon finalization of PPE after the one-time cleansing procedures.

Had the City completed all the necessary procedures for the one-time cleansing of its PPE accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006, the unaccounted discrepancies between the RPCPPE and the accounting books amounting to P731.690 million, as well as other lapses in the monitoring and accounting of PPE, could have been corrected.

We recommended that Management direct the:
IC to-strictly comply with the guidelines and procedures in inventory taking provided under Item 6.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, to wit:
Complete the tagging of PPE counted with pre-numbered stickers;
State clearly in the Inventory Count Form (ICF), RPCPPE or equivalent inventory working papers the actual condition of the various PPE counted, such as in good condition, needing repair, unserviceable, obsolete, no longer needed etc., to serve as basis of the GSD in the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP); and
Ensure that PPE Found at Station and Non-Existing/Missing PPE are labeled as such in the “Remarks” column of the ICF/RPCPPE to facilitate the preparation of the relevant reports by the GSD;
GSD and CAD to continue to coordinate and conduct reconciliation of the results of physical inventory and accounting records and of their respective Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards as well, and identify and address the nature and causes of the variance amounting to P731.690 million;
GSD to continue to comply with its responsibilities under Item 6.3.1 of the subject COA Circular, specifically as follows:
Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with PAR and that these PARs are updated or renewed accordingly;
Prepare reports such as the List of PPE Found at Station, List of Non-Existing/Missing PPE and IIRUP for PPE described as such in the ICF/RPCPPE submitted by the IC, to enable the CAD to take up the necessary adjusting entries for these properties;
CAD to continue to comply with its responsibilities under Item 6.3.2 of the said Circular, specifically to record the necessary adjustments, after completion of proper reconciliation with GSD, including to recognize PPE found at station and set up corresponding receivables from concerned accountable officers for missing PPE to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts; and
GSD, CAD and other concerned offices to proceed with the procedures in the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE, including those without available record of accountability and accounting pursuant to Items 7.0 and 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, taking also into consideration the sunset provision in the Circular that the procedures could no longer be used to further derecognize missing properties and/or cleanse subsequent discrepancies or unreconciled balances in PPE accounts.

Management comments/actions:
The City already started the process and procedures in the one-time cleansing of account and were now validating all items not found at the station and reconciling with our existing records. Additionally, the City was in close coordination with the City of Taguig and DepEd for properties located in EMBO barangays and schools.
The Internal Audit Services (IAS) also conducted an audit of management and operations of inventory related processes that aims to address all previous COA’s audit observations and recommendations which included the following:
Process of delivery and proper notification to the concerned offices/agency
Tagging of properties upon delivery and inspection prior to the acceptance
Establishment of centralized warehouse and safeguarding of asset
Compliance with the three-signatory rule of RA No. 11032 on the Ease of Doing Business
Check-and-balance mechanism on the recording and conduct of physical count
Establishment of internal guidelines, policies and flowchart of the City processes
Reconciliation and avoidance of variance per books and physical count
A continuous coordination was being done by CAD and GSD to ensure that the PPE recently acquired will be properly reflected in the City’s Books of Accounts as well as in the Report of Physical Count. This was to avoid additional discrepancy from the previous years. The said departments were also continuously reconciling the prior year’s procurement to lessen or eliminate the discrepancies.

Furthermore, the CAD and GSD were in the process of developing an automated inventory system in the recording and monitoring of supplies and properties of the City.

The City will also revisit all the PAR issued to the accountable officers and renew/update those PAR which already prescribed the three-year requirement.

The City strengthened its monitoring through the submission of MIR with complete supporting documents (e.g. Notice of Delivery, Purchase Order, Requisition and Issue Slip, etc.) of all departments/ offices.
observations.reason: As per Management, the GSD, the CAD, and the IC are continuously coordinating with each other in the reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records so that the reasons for the discrepancy will be identified. The reconciliation of PPE accounts per book and per physical count was being done all year-round. However, the reconciliation was tedious considering that the volume of unreconciled PPE accounts pertained to those acquired prior to CY 2012, and the limitations on the availability of the documents on file.

observations.managementAction: The City will ensure the feasibility and exigency of the PPAs to be charged against the LDRRMF through strengthening market research/ analysis prior to investment programming and budgeting and intensifying our efforts to assess necessity and implementability of the PPAs through the Program Review and Planning Workshop for DRRM and other platforms for LDRRM planning and budgeting.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Urban Development Department  to institute control measures to ensure that the data and information in the electronic copy of CC expenditures tagged Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and those encoded from AIP Form to CCET Analysis Tool are accurate and complete prior to submission to the CCC to facilitate informed decision-making by concerned city and/or national government officials on matters concerning identification, prioritization, monitoring, evaluation, reporting of, and budgeting for CC PPAs.
observations.managementAction:

Furthermore, the Department will ensure that all relevant AIPs, including those supported by other funding sources such as the SEF, will be properly reflected in upcoming reports.

The Department has issued guidance to its Coordinators involved in the preparation of AIP, emphasizing the need for heighten diligence in reviewing and validating climate change-related PPAs.

The UDD, in coordination with the Local Finance Committee (LFC) and the Makati Climate Change Committee (MCCC) will ensure that all identified and prioritized climate change PPAs are accurately and consistently tagged in the AIP. These measures reflect the Department’s ongoing commitment to improving internal processes and aligning with national climate reporting standards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Makati Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) Core Team oversee the process of tagging climate change responsive PPAs to ensure the appropriateness and consistency of the identified and prioritized climate PPAs.

The adjusted balance of premium contributions and loan amortizations amounting to P851.077 were remitted to the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) within the required period, in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No. 8291, except for the amount of P2.886 million withheld in CY 2024 which was still the subject of pending clarification with the GSIS before remittance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Accounting Department and the HRDO to:
Continue to comply with the relevant provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 8291 by ensuring the timely remittance of valid employee and government agency contributions and deductions to the GSIS within the first ten days of the calendar month following the month in which the contributions and deductions were made; and
Promptly remit the remaining balance of contributions and other deductions for CY 2024 amounting to P2.886 million, after thorough validation and clarification with GSIS.
observations.managementAction:
The CAD and the HRDO have been working closely with the GSIS to address the issues related to non-remittance.  The following processes have been put in place to facilitate reconciliation with the GSIS:
The CAD identifies employees with premium contributions and loan amortizations that were not accepted for payment through the GSIS Electronic Billing and Collection System (eBCS). These lists are forwarded to the HRDO on a monthly basis for review and verification.
The HRDO cross-checks the employees updated service records with the City against those reflected in the GSIS database.  Any discrepancies found are immediately reported to the GSIS for correction.  However, updates involving additional documents from the concerned employees may take time, as these require the employees’ cooperation.
Once the necessary updates have been made, the CAD proceeds with the remittance of the pending contributions and amortizations.  Items that can no longer be remitted are adjusted in the books of accounts either as cancellations of payables or as refunds of loan payments to the concerned employees.
Based on the CAD and HRDO’s initial reconciliation of the remaining balance of the account amounting to P1,988,840.57, it was noted that some items pertain to loan amortizations that are no longer accepted by the GSIS. This is primarily due to the loans being fully paid or restructured by the employees concerned. Once reconciliation and verification are completed, appropriate adjustments will be made in the books of accounts and remit the applicable balance to the GSIS.
observations.reason: Submission of updated information of the City employees is on-going.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the CAD and CTD consistently withhold the required taxes from personnel compensation, as well as from payments to suppliers and contractors, and ensure timely remittance to the BIR.
We also advised the City Treasurer, as the withholding agent and liable officer, to confer with the BIR officials to discuss and potentially appeal any surcharges resulting in the late remittance of withheld taxes.
observations.managementAction:
Out of the P253,258,148.05 taxes withheld as of December 31, 2024, P139,378,151.89 was filed and paid during the return period December 2024. The remaining balance of P113,879,996.16 was remitted on the return periods January and February 2025.
While the City endeavor to file and remit all withholding taxes, including those from accrued expenses, certain transactions are recorded beyond the statutory remittance deadline. As such, withheld taxes from accrued expenses recorded after January 10, 2025, could not be included in the January remittances.
It is emphasized that the delayed remittance of withheld taxes was not intentional, but rather due to timing and operational circumstances.

Partially Implemented | The Internal Audit Services reviewed the acceptance and inspection process for the delivery of goods, supplies and equipment. Currently, the General Services Department is in the process of complying the recommendations and formulating the internal guidelines to strengthen the effectiveness of governance, risk management and control process for inventories and property, plant and equipment.  The General Services Department is further required to simplify the inventory process, so as to comply with   R.A. No. 11032, also known as Ease of Doing Business and Efficient Government Service Delivery Act of 2018.  It also includes establishment of a comprehensive system of monitoring transactions for an efficient turnaround in the delivery of government service, and a 3-signatory rule for accountability purposes.  Further, the Internal Audit Services required the centralized warehouse to be operational at the soonest possible time.


Though the inventory plan was incorporated in the year end closing procedures of the City, for 2021, a plan is being formulated to consider the procedural guidelines which includes the cut-off dates and activities conducted to ensure that there would be sufficient time to reconcile and identify the discrepancies between the physical count and the records in the books of accounts.

Nonetheless, the succeeding inventory count is ensured to be in accordance with the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP.


Reconciliation was made as part of the year end closing procedures. Causes of some variances were identified and supported with proper documentations. While the others represent items, which were recorded back as inventory despite submission of proofs of issuances from prior periods, since these were presented during the physical count. Yet, there is a need to revisit previous documentations to substantiate proper recording.

The GSD and Accounting Department did a reconciliation on a per account basis regularly.


After further review of the existing inventory process, the following control measures are established to efficiently and effectively account all the acquisitions and issuances of supplies and materials and timely record the same in the Books of Accounts so that the possible discrepancy will be eliminated or minimized:

Goods are subject for inspection of the GSD upon delivery
AIR and Notice of Delivery are to be issued thereafter when the specifications were met
These reports and other supporting documents are then forwarded to COA to be stamped received
Copies of such documents are to be submitted to the Accounting Department for proper recording in the books of accounts as part of the Procurement Received Journal

Further, all departments/ offices are required to submit a Monthly Inventory Report as supported by RIS and RSMI to properly account the issuance of supplies and materials.


The submission of Monthly Inventory Report accompanied by AIR/DR, RIS/RSMI and other similar documents is already incorporated in the existing inventory process.  These documents are used as reference in recording the transactions related to the supplies and materials inventory in the books of accounts.

Each office/ department submits a monthly inventory report, the CAD will strictly monitor the submission of complete and updated records of the officers/ custodians on a monthly basis to ensure that their records of inventory are updated based on the Books of Accounts.


The CAD already adjusted in the books of accounts some of the identified reasons for discrepancies which include issuances of the supplies for some of the prior period adjustments of the City.  Also, the CAD identified that there were some supplies and materials which were initially recorded as expense in the books of accounts on the basis of Requisition and Issue Slip and Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued which were recorded back to the inventory at year end to reflect the real balance of the inventory account.  These were the remaining supplies and materials inventory from different departments/offices at year end.


The outstanding balance of P397,994,971.84 is still subject for reconciliation.


Adjustment amounting to P87,763,019.70 relative to the issuance made in 2020, was recognized in the City’s Books of Accounts under Journal Entry Voucher no. 2020-12-0863 with corresponding attachments.


The updated SLC were already submitted to COA via the intranet.  Also, the CAD will ensure to diligently update and submit the SLC to COA.

Since the Internal Audit Services is still in the process of assessing and drafting the guidelines and process flow to ensure effectiveness of governance, risk management and control process relative to the City’s inventories and property, plant and equipment, the CAD will ensure the control mechanism for the recording of deliveries and issuances are still in place.  Nevertheless, once the guidelines were created, the CAD will strictly comply.

Reconciliation was made as part of the year end closing procedures. Causes of some variances were identified and supported with proper documentations. While the others represent items, which were recorded back as inventory despite submission of proofs of issuances from prior periods, since these were presented during the physical count. Yet, there is a need to revisit previous documentations to substantiate proper recording.

The GSD and Accounting Department did a reconciliation on a per account basis regularly.


After further review of the existing inventory process, the following control measures are established to efficiently and effectively account all the acquisitions and issuances of supplies and materials and timely record the same in the Books of Accounts so that the possible discrepancy will be eliminated or minimized:

Goods are subject for inspection of the GSD upon delivery
AIR and Notice of Delivery are to be issued thereafter when the specifications were met
These reports and other supporting documents are then forwarded to COA to be stamped received
Copies of such documents are to be submitted to the Accounting Department for proper recording in the books of accounts as part of the Procurement Received Journal

Further, all departments/ offices are required to submit a Monthly Inventory Report as supported by RIS and RSMI to properly account the issuance of supplies and materials.


The submission of Monthly Inventory Report accompanied by AIR/DR, RIS/RSMI and other similar documents is already incorporated in the existing inventory process.  These documents are used as reference in recording the transactions related to the supplies and materials inventory in the books of accounts.

Each office/ department submits a monthly inventory report, the CAD will strictly monitor the submission of complete and updated records of the officers/ custodians on a monthly basis to ensure that their records of inventory are updated based on the Books of Accounts.


The CAD already adjusted in the books of accounts some of the identified reasons for discrepancies which include issuances of the supplies for some of the prior period adjustments of the City.  Also, the CAD identified that there were some supplies and materials which were initially recorded as expense in the books of accounts on the basis of Requisition and Issue Slip and Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued which were recorded back to the inventory at year end to reflect the real balance of the inventory account.  These were the remaining supplies and materials inventory from different departments/offices at year end.


The outstanding balance of P397,994,971.84 is still subject for reconciliation.


Adjustment amounting to P87,763,019.70 relative to the issuance made in 2020, was recognized in the City’s Books of Accounts under Journal Entry Voucher no. 2020-12-0863 with corresponding attachments.


The updated SLC were already submitted to COA via the intranet.  Also, the CAD will ensure to diligently update and submit the SLC to COA.

Since the Internal Audit Services is still in the process of assessing and drafting the guidelines and process flow to ensure effectiveness of governance, risk management and control process relative to the City’s inventories and property, plant and equipment, the CAD will ensure the control mechanism for the recording of deliveries and issuances are still in place.  Nevertheless, once the guidelines were created, the CAD will strictly comply.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management to require HRDO to:

Adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and other payroll deductions such that the monthly net take home pay of any City employee does not fall below the prescribed ceiling of P5,000.00;





Direct those in charge of payroll preparation to exercise diligence in monitoring net take home pay of City personnel to comply with the requirements of existing law.  Remedial measures should be done to rectify the situation of the 1,288 City personnel receiving below the required net take home pay; and



Strictly implement Section 47 of the General Provisions of RA No. 11465 on the mandatory monthly net take home-pay of P5,000.00.
observations.managementAction:
Fully Implemented







Fully Implemented







Fully Implemented | The City revisited the payrolls and determined that the major causes why several employees are receiving their salaries below the minimum monthly net pay is due to the loans availed from Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) and Makati City Employees and Multi-purpose Cooperative (MACEMCO).

Due to the coordination between the HRDO and GSIS, loans being applied by the employees are only approved after the assigned HRDO personnel confirmed that the monthly take home pay of that individual would not fall below the prescribed ceiling.  However, the loan approval of MACEMCO relies heavily on pay slip submitted to them.  As such, there is a possibility that loan transactions from other government agencies after the date appearing on the pay slip are not considered.

Accordingly, the MACEMCO will coordinate with the City HRDO all loans availed by the concerned employees if base on their assessment, the loans applied will result to the decrease in minimum monthly net pay below the requirements of the law.

As part of the remedial measures, it was agreed to adjust the monthly net take home pay of all concerned employees by reducing the amount to be deducted from the monthly amortization of loans availed                     from MACEMCO effective                                   May 16 to 31, 2021 pay period.

Furthermore, the HRDO and CAD will diligently monitor the net take home pay of the City personnel to comply with the requirements of existing laws.

The management set an internal control measure to monitor compliance with the implementing guidelines effective immediately.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
City Accounting Department (CAD), in coordination with the General Services Department (GSD) and the Department of Engineering and Public Works (DEPW), gather the documents necessary in updating or reconstructing their records to be able to provide depreciation to the subject PPE items; and
CAD effect the necessary adjustments of the depreciation expense for the year, accumulated depreciation for prior years and the corresponding amount to the government equity account.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the GSD to conduct actual physical inventory before year-end in collaboration with all department heads and offices, listing all properties found and include the same; and
Create an inventory reconciliation team from the CAD and the GSD to identify the details of the discrepancies and work back on prior years’ transactions to complete the reconciliation process and reduce the discrepancies.

Set-up effective and efficient controls over denied, returned and untimely filed claims such as but not limited to:
Come up with policies detailing the procedural guidelines in the assessment, review and filing of claims to PHIC to ensure that these are compliant, accurate, complete and filed in a timely manner;
Define the responsibilities of those in charge thereof, including the imposition of appropriate sanctions for unjustified failure to properly discharge such duties;
Preparation of monthly monitoring report on the status of outstanding PHIC claims; and
Preparation of evaluation report to identify the causes of denied, returned and untimely filed claims and the person/s responsible thereof.
Evaluate the collectability of receivables from PHIC based on factors such as aging of accounts, collection experience, identified doubtful accounts, etc., and set-up provisions for impairment; and
Evaluate dormant accounts, if any, for possible write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management continue their efforts and activities that contribute to the realization and attainment of the income targets while undertaking necessary measures to intensify collection of income accounts with unrealized proportions.

We recommended that Management review the existing policies as regards the operations of OsMak and apply the necessary changes to improve its profitability including collection system and procedures and conduct a periodic review of the LEE’s financial performance.  Otherwise, OsMak should not be considered as a local economic enterprise and its functions and services should be reintegrated into the regular LGU operations in accordance with LBC No. 111.

We recommended that Management:
Conduct adequate procurement planning taking into consideration the school supplies on hand in the estimation of the quantity of  items to be procured for the coming school year to avoid excessive purchases;
Consider distributing the school supplies to students in public schools of Makati City for school year 2020-2021 to avoid further deterioration and wastage of government funds; and
Direct the Schools Division Superintendent to promptly monitor the implementation of the identified programs, projects and activities of the DepEd – Makati City.

We recommended that Management:
Utilize the 20% DF and ensure that all the programmed projects are implemented;
Assure the completion of prior years’ unfinished PPAs, determine the causes of delayed implementation and impose sanctions to defaulting contractors, if warranted; and
Ensure that PPAs under the 20% DF are responsive to the community’s needs and implementable in order that funds can be optimally utilized pursuant to the intent of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01 dated 
February 17, 2017.

We recommend that Management:
Encourage optimum utilization of the amount appropriated for GAD to fully achieve the desired objectives; and
Coordinate with the implementing offices to ensure the effective implementation of GAD PPAs for successful delivery of the benefits to the constituents.

The CT/Collecting Officers cease the issuance of ORs for collections thru ACPS to present fairly the amount of collections and deposits made; prepare a separate list/report for collections directly deposited by the payors to the bank thru ACPS and submit to the AD; and











The AD record directly to the account Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account all collections made thru ACPS to avoid preparation of adjusting entry at the end of each month.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Institute necessary measures so that the properties without TCT will be processed and individual TCT will be issued. We recommended that Management provide depreciation for the Work/Zoo Animals and Other Infrastructure Assets accounts.

We recommended that the DepEd Superintendent and General Services Officer:
Make a demand from the AOs concerned to pay the money value of the lost items, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor, for reference and monitoring; and
Determine the correct money value, and submit the following as basis for appraisal by the Technical Service Office of COA:
list and description including book value;
Date of acquisition
Property number
Account classification;
Condition of the property; and
Other additional relevant information of the properties lost duly certified by the general services officer, municipal or barangay treasurer, as the case may be.

We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant:
Return immediately the excess/unspent fund for completed projects/program, to settle obligations to the source agency pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994;
Coordinate with the Accounting Office of the concerned source agency to request the  details of the NGAs’ receivables from the City of Makati to expedite the reconciliation of the discrepancy of P780,653.53; and
Henceforth, transferred funds should be utilized in accordance with the agreement and should not remain idle so that the intended beneficiaries will not be deprived of the benefits of the program.

We recommended that the City Mayor requires the:
MSWDO, as the Focal Person, to ensure that the Supplementary Feeding Program is immediately implemented upon receipt of funds; and
Concerned City officials to seek authority from the source agency to utilize the fund beyond the agreed period, otherwise, return the fund to the source agency or national treasury as provided in COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
We recommended that Management ensure that the 20% Development Fund are utilized for the purposes intended, as cited in the JMC.
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the MDRRMO continue to monitor implementation of projects to attain 100 percent completion.
We recommended that the BAC and BAC Secretariat ensure that the NTP are issued within 7 days from the approval of the contract to facilitate the implementation of the projects.
We recommended that Management ensure the immediate implementation of the remaining three (3) programs.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs as required under JMC No. 2013-01 and to conduct regular coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that programs are undertaken within the target period.

observations.recommendation:
1.12 In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Create an appraisal committee who will work for the fair valuation of
all land without costs and require the Accounting Department to
record them in the books and the GSD to update the RPCPPE;
b. Require the Accounting Department and GSD to collaborate in the
review of prior years transactions concerning land account and
completion of the reconciliation process. Consequently, record the
necessary adjustments in the books in accordance with PPSAS 17;
c. Require the GSD and Accounting Department in coordination with
the Urban Development Department (UDD) and Assessment
Department to conduct ocular inspection of various land and note the
exact location and actual use of properties without details or lacking
sufficient details in the accounting records and RPCPPE to enable
updating of records and reports; and
d. Direct the GSD to verify ownership of land without titles with the
Register of Deeds (RD) or Land Registration Authority (LRA).
Accordingly, seek for the issuance of titles in the name of the City
and/or undertake the necessary action on the results of verification
with the RD and LRA.

2.9 We recommended the following courses of action:
a. The Inventory Committee conduct a complete and thorough physical
inventory of these PPE accounts together with the corresponding
Land account and list all City properties in the RPCPPE prior to
reconciliation with the accounting records;
b. The Accounting Department in coordination with the GSD and
DEPW gather relevant documents and complete details of subject
PPE items and take up the necessary adjustments on depreciation and
accumulated depreciation; and
c. The Accounting Department initiate the appraisal of subject
properties, including the Poblacion Public Market, if efforts to locate
and retrieve previous records of the accounts proved futile, in order to
determine the current fair values of the properties for subsequent
adjustment of the PPE, depreciation expense and other related
accounts.

3.11 We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct actual physical inventory
before year-end in collaboration with all department heads and
offices, listing all properties found and include them in the RPCPPE;
and
b. Create an inventory reconciliation team from the Accounting
Department and GSD that will identify the details of the
discrepancies and work back on prior years transactions in order to
complete the reconciliation process and reduce the discrepancies to a
minimum level.

4.18 We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
a. The City Accountant to take up reversing entries restoring the
balances of RPT and SET Receivable accounts representing
delinquent accounts aged ten years or more that have been written off
in 2016;
b. The City Treasurer to submit to the Sanggunian concerned the
certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained
uncollected or unpaid for at least one year and a statement of the
reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment, on or
before December 31st of the year immediately succeeding the year in
which the delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in
the enforcement of the remedies for collection, as required in Section
269 of R.A. 7160;
c. The City Treasurer to apply administrative and judicial remedies
provided for under R.A. 7160 that will enforce collection of delinquent
taxes; and
d. The City Assessor to make representation with the Register of Deeds
to inform the Assessment Department on a continuing basis, of real
property listed in its registry, to allow periodic updating of records of
real properties in the assessment roll for any change in ownership or
alienation, accompanied by copies of corresponding deeds of sale,
donation or other forms of alienation.

5.5 We recommended that:
a. The City Mayor require the City Assessor to prepare an updated
SFMVs as basis for the conduct of a general revision of real property
assessments, and cause the drafting of an appropriate ordinance for
consideration of the Sanggunian; and
b. The Presiding Officer/Members of Sanggunian calendar, deliberate
and cause passage of an Ordinance incorporating the updated SFMVs
prepared by the City Assessor and to appropriate funds for the
conduct of the general revision of Real Property Assessments.

6.11 We recommended that the Accounting Department –
a. Apply perpetual inventory method and the accounting standards
and policies of recording acquisition of supplies and materials and
subsequent recognition of expense consistently in accordance with
the provisions of PPSAS 12 and the accounting policy on inventories
adopted by the City;
b. Review charges to the food supplies expense and take up necessary
adjustments to reclassify to the appropriate expense accounts those
incorrectly charged to the said account; and
c. Reconcile and analyze the discrepancy of P2,296,108.51 between the
balances reported in the books and those per physical count to
establish accountability for the difference. Conduct investigation if
warranted and make the required adjustment. Reconciliation should
be clearly documented to indicate that review has been done and
communicated for correction and/or adjustments.

7.15 We recommended that the Accounting Department:
a. Take up the required adjustments on repairs and maintenance, PPE
and related accounts for capitalizable costs of P6,318,313.84. Review
all other charges to repairs and maintenance expense for the year and
make the necessary adjustments to conform with the recognition
principle in PPSAS 17; and
b. Reclassify items totalling P1,218,000.00 incorrectly taken up as repairs
and maintenance expense to supplies and materials inventory account,
and record subsequent adjustment to related expense accounts upon
use or distribution.

8.11 In view thereof, we recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Direct the Accounting Department to scrutinize and review the
accounts of retired and resigned City employees for the purpose of
determining if the time-bounded and specific undertakings have been
completed or not, as basis for further action, if warranted;
b. Require the Law Department to apply the remedies provided by law
and regulations to demand full liquidation of outstanding cash
advances from concerned officers and employees of the City and from
those who have already retired and resigned from service; and
c. Require appropriate clearance from money and property
accountability from resigning or retiring officials and employees.
Failure to do so will make the approving authority jointly and
solidarily liable with the persons primarily accountable pursuant to
Sections 102 and 104 of P.D. 1445.

9.9 We recommended that Management strictly implement the rules and
regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances
and PC, stated in COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2015-009 and in the
Citys own policies/guidelines. Thus, it should:
a. Require full liquidation of PC by designated AOs at year-end. Direct
all AOs to comply with the year-end liquidation of all cash advances
and PC within 20 days after the end of the year and refund of
unexpended balances;
b. Demand immediate liquidation of outstanding PC granted prior to
2016 amounting to P369,230.00 by requiring submission of proper
accounting of expenditures incurred and refund of unspent balance;
c. Stop the practice of using PC for planned activities and for expenses
and purchases of huge amounts or those exceeding P15,000 which can
be conveniently paid by check; and
d. Review each PC granted and accordingly reduce them to reasonable
amounts taking into consideration the petty cash requirements for one
month. Also, consider reducing the number of PC custodians to what
is actually needed for the Citys operations and in accordance with its
guidelines.

10.11 We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Stop the payment of supervisory, committee and regular
employees honoraria to UMak officials;
b. Cause the refund of payments made to UMak officials for the
years 2015 and 2016 totaling P7,544,926.72; and
c. Follow the guidelines in the Manual on Position Classification
and Compensation in compensating faculty members performing
supervisory functions and workload other than teaching.

11.10 In view thereof, we recommended that the City Mayor and the
Sangguniang Panlungsod:
a. Restrain the UMak President from entering into agreements or
contracts with private institutions or organizations without the
express approval of the Sangguniang Panlungsod. The City Mayor
as Chairman of the Board of Regents, as authorized by the
Sanggunian should represent the City in all its transactions and sign
in behalf of the City, all contracts or agreements;
b. Regulate and control the actions of UMak officials such that these
do not infringe the intent and primary purpose of its creation as
stated in City Ordinance No. 99-126;
c. Initiate the review of any and all contracts or agreements entered
into by the UMak President and other officials for and in behalf of
the University, with private organizations to determine propriety
and legality of such action in relation to its mandate; and
d. Ensure that all contracts, agreements and related undertakings to
be entered into or pursued by UMak involving government funds
and property have the approval/concurrence of the Sangguniang
Panlungsod.

12.5 We recommended and Management agreed to:
On DILG Fund Transfers -
a. Communicate with and inform DILG of the unspent fund transfers,
request for a copy of the MOA and inquire about the real intent of
the said financial subsidies or the conditions agreed upon in the
implementation of the proposed projects. We further
recommended that in case DILG desires to have these subsidies
returned due to non-implementation, Management immediately
cause the return of the funds to DILG. Otherwise, with the
concurrence of DILG, the accance of P2,100,000 may be
transferred to the general fund;
b. Seek the written approval of DILG so that the unexpended balance
of WACS amounted to P26,146.87 may be transferred to the
general fund;
c. Hasten the full implementation and maximum utilization of the
funds for MAP considering that the program is aligned to the
medical and social welfare services of the City;
On DOH Fund Transfers -
d. Request for a written approval from the DOH for the extension of
all program implementations until such time as intended and
coordinate on the treatment of the remaining balances of the fund;
and
e. Formulate the Citys plan of action to implement the said programs
in line with its own health programs.
On DENR Fund Transfers -
f. We recommended that City officials concerned obtain copy of the
agreement or confer with DENR to clarify the intended program
and devise a plan of action for its utilization or refund the fund
balance as determined to be necessary.
On DOST Fund Transfers -
g. We recommended that the UMak President and officials concerned
resolve or decide whether or not the program will be carried out.
In the affirmative, they should seek advice from ICTO to facilitate
immediate implementation of the program, otherwise, the amount
received should be refunded.

13.5 We recommended that the City Treasurer remit the five percent share
from building permit fee collections to the Bureau of Treasury for the
account of the DPWH, immediately on the succeeding month following
the date of collection.

14.26 We recommended that the City thru the EEMO to:
a. Immediately execute lease contracts embodying the terms and
conditions of the lease to be observed by the contracting parties, the
rate of rent including additional fees for better located stalls and
penalties for failure to pay within the prescribed period, in
accordance with the provisions of the Revised Revenue Code;
b. Initiate the posting of notice of vacancy of vacant stalls at the
Poblacion Public Market, to attract prospective lessees, thereby
generating revenues therefrom that will help sustain its operations
and improve market condition; and
c. Issue an official receipts as evidence of payment of rental of
extension space instead of cash tickets. The rental of extension
space should be specifically stated in the lease contract and
included in the fixed monthly rental, to be collected in accordance
with the provisions of the Revised Revenue Code or related
amendments, if any.

15.11 We therefore recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Require the School Board, that in the preparation of the annual
budget, to confine its programs, projects and activities chargeable
to SEF, to those expressly enumerated in Section 272 of RA 7160
and Section 1 of RA 5447;
b. Limit the utilization of SEF to the intended purpose authorized by
law; and
c. Restore from the General Fund the amount of expenditures
improperly charged against SEF.

16.6 In view thereof, we recommended that the City Mayor restrain DepEd
officials and members of the Local School Board from including in the
budget for SEF those expenditures that are not among the activities
provided by laws and regulations and consequently charging the same
against SEF.

17.17 We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Require the DepEd Schools Division Superintendent to make a
formal report regarding the IT equipment already lost, the
persons accountable/liable, and evaluation on the extent of their
liability for the loss and the action taken by the Office for such
loss. Also, require the DepEd Schools Division Superintendent
to assume command responsibility for the proper safekeeping of
equipment issued by the City;
b. Compel the school property custodians through the DepEd
Schools Division Superintendent to account and make a report
on the status of the 153 laptop computers and 53 study book
tablets which were not presented during the physical inventory,
and identify persons accountable for the loss;
c. Require school property custodians to turn over to the DepEd
property custodian excess IT equipment so that these may be
issued to other Makati schools in need;
d. Require the DepEd Schools Division Superintendent to institute
control measures for the proper care and safekeeping of IT
equipment;
e. Initiate recovery of the money value of lost IT equipment and
administrative and/or legal action against erring accountable
DepEd personnel;
f. Require GSD that in the succeeding conduct of physical
inventory, to include all relevant information in the RPCPPE
regarding existence and actual condition of the assets; and
g. Confer with DepEd officials, to require all retiring and
resigning DepEd personnel assigned in the City to secure
clearance from property accountability from GSD and City
officials concerned prior to transfer to another government
office or upon retirement, as a pre-requisite for the issuance of
DepEd clearance. They should be required to return all City
properties under their accountability as a requisite for the
issuance of City clearance.
